creator_thesis: @etymologynerd creates infographic-based content that traces the word origins of everyday objects, places, and concepts — showing how language acts as a compressed archive of human history, culture, and migration. The creator's thesis is that etymology is universally accessible and endlessly surprising: virtually any domain (food, geography, science, music) reveals hidden linguistic patterns once you look. Most recently, this framework has been extended into a book arguing that social media algorithms are the newest force reshaping how language evolves.

key_concepts.name: Etymology as hidden history
key_concepts.definition: Words function as compressed records of cultural exchange, human migration, and historical discovery, making linguistics a form of archaeology.

key_concepts.name: Toponym patterns
key_concepts.definition: Place names encode layers of colonial, indigenous, and immigrant history, making geography a readable map of power and settlement.

key_concepts.name: Scientific naming conventions
key_concepts.definition: Taxonomy, anatomy, medicine, and geology follow systematic Latin and Greek construction rules that preserve the logic of early scientific thought.

key_concepts.name: Algospeak
key_concepts.definition: Platform content moderation and recommendation algorithms now actively drive vocabulary formation, producing terms like 'unalive' as users adapt language to evade filters.

key_concepts.name: Brand and cultural naming
key_concepts.definition: Artist names, art movement labels, typeface names, and currency terms reflect the values, aesthetics, and power structures of the era that coined them.

key_concepts.name: Cross-domain universality
key_concepts.definition: The same etymological mechanisms — metaphor, borrowing, eponymy — operate identically across unrelated fields, suggesting language change follows deep structural patterns.

key_concepts.name: Visual/spatial etymology mapping
key_concepts.definition: Presenting word origins as infographics and geographic maps makes abstract linguistic history legible and shareable for general audiences.

references.name: ALGOSPEAK: How Social Media is Transforming the Future of Language

positions: Algorithms are now an active linguistic force: platform moderation produces vocabulary ('unalive', 'skibidi', 'cottagecore') the same way trade routes and conquest once did.
Etymology is universally accessible — any domain reveals hidden linguistic patterns once you apply the same lens.
Words carry compressed cultural records: to know a word's origin is to recover a piece of history that written records may have lost.
Visual presentation (infographics, maps) is not just a delivery format but an argument — it demonstrates that etymology has spatial and systemic logic, not just trivia value.
